title: Clinical Evaluation of Gonadal Dysgenesis: Five-Case Resident Training Module
subtitle: A Resident Training Module: genetic → gonadal → phenotypic sex; Complete Gonadal Dysgenesis (Swyer) and Mixed Gonadal Dysgenesis (MGD) — hCG stimulation, mosaicism, FISH for idic(Y), CAH misdiagnosis trap, laparoscopic histopathology, and gonadoblastoma risk
category: Pediatric Endocrinology / Disorders of Sex Development
authors: Resident training module — five published case reports (see sourceFile)
sourceFile: Errahali Y et al. Cureus 2026 e100735; Saikia UK et al. J Hum Reprod Sci 2019;12(2):169-172; Thukral A et al. Indian J Endocrinol Metab 2013;17(Suppl1):S268; Shetty RA et al. J Reprod Infertil 2024;25(1):72-76; Monolov et al. Clin Case Rep 2024;12(8):e9318

relatedChapters.title: Leydig Cell Hypoplasia (LCH): Two Published Cases of LHCGR Mutations

patient.sex: Male and female phenotypes; 45,X mosaic to 46,XY karyotypes
patient.chiefComplaint: Gonadal dysgenesis — five published cases spanning Swyer syndrome, MGD, mosaicism, and diagnostic mimics
pmh: Case 1 (Errahali 2026): 16F phenotype, primary amenorrhea, Tanner 2, 46,XY — Swyer syndrome
Case 2 (Saikia 2019): 2yo reared female, Prader 2, initial 45,X mosaic, hCG-responsive testosterone — MGD
Case 3 (Thukral 2013): 16yo reared female, misdiagnosed CAH on empiric steroids, 46,XY MGD
Case 4 (Shetty 2024): 27M phenotype, azoospermia, micropenis, uterus on imaging, 45,X/46,XY/46,X,idic(Yp) mosaic
Case 5 (Monolov 2024): 16F, primary amenorrhea, stunting, hypergonadotropic hypogonadism, 46,XY CGD

caseDescription: Five-case resident training module on gonadal dysgenesis using a glassbox sequence: Genetic Sex → Gonadal Sex → Phenotypic Sex. Case 1 — Complete gonadal dysgenesis (Swyer): 16-year-old phenotypic female, primary amenorrhea, hypergonadotropic hypogonadism, uterus present, 46,XY; bilateral gonadectomy then estrogen-progesterone (Errahali 2026). Case 2 — MGD with atypical mosaicism: 2-year-old Prader 2, initial 45,X mosaic, hCG-stimulated testosterone rise, laparoscopy showing streak left gonad and dysgenetic right testis (Saikia 2019). Case 3 — MGD diagnostic mimic: 16-year-old previously misdiagnosed with CAH on empiric steroids; normal cortisol and 17-OHP; 46,XY with testicular histology only (Thukral 2013). Case 4 — complex mosaic with idic(Yp): 27-year-old phenotypic male, azoospermia, micropenis, uterus, FISH 45,X[44]/46,XY[5]/46,X,idic(Yp)[1] (Shetty 2024). Case 5 — CGD and tumor surveillance: 16-year-old female, markedly elevated LH/FSH, unicornuate uterus, 46,XY; prophylactic gonadectomy and HRT (Monolov 2024).
keyFindings: Trace discordance at genetic, gonadal, or phenotypic nodes — not phenotype alone
Swyer (46,XY CGD): no functional testis → no testosterone or AMH → Müllerian structures persist → female phenotype
MGD: asymmetric gonads (streak + dysgenetic testis); mosaic karyotype common; hCG may prove functional testicular tissue
Never start empiric CAH steroids without cortisol and 17-OHP confirmation
Y-containing dysgenetic gonads carry gonadoblastoma/dysgerminoma risk (~20–30% in CGD) — gonadectomy standard
Complex mosaicism and idic(Y) require FISH beyond standard karyotype
finalDiagnosis: Teaching module — five distinct gonadal dysgenesis pathways: (1) 46,XY complete gonadal dysgenesis (Swyer); (2–3) 46,XY/45,X mixed gonadal dysgenesis with diagnostic challenges; (4) MGD with structural Y mosaicism and retained Müllerian structures in an adult male phenotype; (5) 46,XY complete gonadal dysgenesis with oncologic surveillance emphasis. Core lesson: sequential DSD evaluation with biochemistry, cytogenetics, hCG stimulation, high-resolution FISH when indicated, and laparoscopic histopathology when non-invasive data conflict.
learningObjectives: Apply the genetic → gonadal → phenotypic sex framework to gonadal dysgenesis
Distinguish complete gonadal dysgenesis (Swyer) from mixed gonadal dysgenesis (MGD)
Interpret hCG stimulation when karyotype and imaging are discordant
Avoid the CAH misdiagnosis trap — confirm biochemistry before empiric steroids
Recognize oncologic indications for prophylactic gonadectomy in Y-bearing dysgenetic gonads
Use FISH to resolve complex mosaicism and isodicentric Y chromosomes

items.title: Why DSD evaluation must follow genetic → gonadal → phenotypic sex
items.content:
Gonadal dysgenesis evaluation requires a sequential glassbox approach: Genetic Sex (karyotype, FISH, microarray) → Gonadal Sex (palpation, AMH, inhibin B, hCG stimulation, laparoscopy) → Phenotypic Sex (external genitalia Prader/EMS, internal structures on ultrasound/MRI).

Discordance at any node disrupts fetal differentiation deterministically. A 46,XY karyotype does not guarantee testicular function; a female phenotype does not exclude Y-chromosomal gonads; initial mosaic karyotype may underrepresent Y-lineage cells. This module's five cases map failures at each node.
keyPoints: Discordance at any level drives the workup.
Never stop at phenotype or karyotype alone.

items.title: How complete gonadal dysgenesis (Swyer syndrome) produces a female phenotype in 46,XY individuals
items.content:
Swyer syndrome = complete gonadal dysgenesis in 46,XY individuals: total failure of testicular differentiation. Without functional Sertoli cells, no AMH → Müllerian structures (uterus, fallopian tubes, upper vagina) persist. Without functional Leydig cells, no fetal testosterone or DHT → external genitalia develop along the female default pathway.

Presentation: primary amenorrhea, hypergonadotropic hypogonadism (high LH/FSH, low estradiol/testosterone), uterus present, streak gonads instead of ovaries. Case 1 (Errahali 2026): 16-year-old phenotypic female, Tanner 2, primary amenorrhea, 46,XY — mechanistic baseline for the module.

items.title: Case 1: Swyer syndrome — 16-year-old with primary amenorrhea and 46,XY karyotype
items.content:
Errahali et al. (2026), Cureus. 16-year-old phenotypic female: pubertal delay, Tanner stage 2 secondary sexual characteristics, primary amenorrhea. Hormones: hypergonadotropic hypogonadism. Imaging: uterus and vaginal cavity present; ovaries not visualized. Cytogenetics: 46,XY.

Management: high malignancy risk in dysgenetic Y-bearing gonads → bilateral laparoscopic gonadectomy, then estrogen-progesterone induction of puberty. Ref: Cureus 18(1) e100735.

items.title: How mixed gonadal dysgenesis differs from complete gonadal dysgenesis
items.content:
Mixed gonadal dysgenesis (MGD) typically features asymmetric gonadal development — often a streak gonad on one side and a dysgenetic testis on the other — usually in the setting of mosaicism (45,X/46,XY most common). Phenotype ranges from ambiguous genitalia (Prader 2–4) to male or female appearance depending on proportion of functional testicular tissue and androgen exposure.

Unlike Swyer (bilateral streak gonads, female phenotype), MGD may have partial androgen effect and functional Leydig/Sertoli tissue on one side. hCG stimulation may raise testosterone (Case 2). Surgical exploration with histopathology is gold standard when imaging and karyotype conflict.
keyPoints: Partial androgen effect possible — ambiguous genitalia.

items.title: Case 2: MGD in a 2-year-old — when hCG stimulation contradicts initial karyotype
items.content:
Saikia et al. (2019), J Hum Reprod Sci. 2-year-old reared female: ambiguous genitalia Prader score 2 since birth, nonpalpable gonads, normal growth. Initial cytogenetics: 45,X mosaicism. CAH excluded biochemically. Post-hCG stimulation: significant testosterone elevation — proving functional testicular tissue despite nonpalpable gonads and initial mosaic karyotype.

Laparoscopy: bilateral gonads in ovarian fossa, hypoplastic uterus, bilateral fallopian tubes. Left gonad = streak; right gonad = seminiferous tubules and Leydig cells. Pearl: when imaging inconclusive and biochemistry contradicts karyotype, laparoscopy + histopathology is definitive.
keyPoints: hCG raised testosterone → testicular tissue present.
Laparoscopy: streak left, dysgenetic testis right.

items.title: Why Case 3 exposes the empiric CAH steroid trap in ambiguous genitalia
items.content:
Thukral et al. (2013), Indian J Endocrinol Metab. 16-year-old reared female: primary amenorrhea, genital ambiguity, absent secondary sexual characteristics. Previously misdiagnosed with CAH and treated empirically with glucocorticoids without foundational biochemical proof.

Re-evaluation: normal cortisol and normal 17-hydroxyprogesterone — CAH excluded. Karyotype: 46,XY. Surgical exploration: histopathology showed only testicular tissue (atrophic seminiferous tubules, Leydig cell hyperplasia); no ovarian tissue — ovotesticular DSD ruled out. Diagnosis: 46,XY MGD. Lesson: never initiate steroid therapy for ambiguous genitalia without cortisol, electrolytes, and 17-OHP confirmation.
keyPoints: Empiric CAH steroids without biochemistry — error.

items.title: Case 4: Adult MGD with azoospermia, uterus, and isodicentric Y mosaicism
items.content:
Shetty et al. (2024), J Reprod Infertil. 27-year-old phenotypic male: azoospermia, micropenis, bilateral non-palpable testes. Imaging: small uterus present. Cytogenetics + FISH: complex mosaic 45,X[44]/46,XY[5]/46,X,idic(Yp)[1].

Pathophysiology: dominant 45,X line plus unstable isodicentric Y disrupted gonadal maturation → retained Müllerian structures and complete spermatogenic failure despite male rearing. Pearl: adult azoospermia with micropenis and cryptorchidism warrants high-resolution FISH to map structural Y abnormalities beyond standard karyotype.
keyPoints: FISH essential for structural Y defects.

items.title: Case 5: Complete gonadal dysgenesis — tumor risk and hormone replacement
items.content:
Monolov et al. (2024), Clin Case Rep. 16-year-old female: primary amenorrhea, constitutional stunting. Hormones: markedly elevated gonadotropins (LH 35.0, FSH 86.6 mIU/mL), low estradiol and testosterone. Imaging: uterine hypoplasia (unicornuate uterus with rudimentary horn); ovaries unidentifiable. Karyotype: 46,XY.

Complete gonadal dysgenesis with strict oncologic emphasis: approximately 20–30% of 46,XY dysgenetic gonads develop gonadoblastoma or dysgerminoma. Prophylactic gonadectomy is immediate standard of care on diagnosis, followed by cyclical estrogen-progesterone for menarche induction and bone mineral density optimization. Ref: Clin Case Rep 12(8) e9318.

items.title: Why prophylactic gonadectomy is standard in 46,XY gonadal dysgenesis
items.content:
Dysgenetic gonads containing Y-chromosomal material carry substantial risk of gonadoblastoma and dysgerminoma — reported approximately 20–30% in complete gonadal dysgenesis (Case 5). Risk exists even when gonads are intra-abdominal streaks and non-palpable. Bilateral gonadectomy upon diagnosis is standard oncologic care before or coordinated with pubertal hormone induction.

Cases 1 and 5 both emphasize laparoscopic bilateral gonadectomy followed by estrogen-progesterone replacement. Surveillance alone is inadequate when malignancy risk is this high and gonads are non-functional for fertility.
keyPoints: Gonadectomy before/delayed with HRT — not surveillance alone.

items.title: How hCG stimulation and laparoscopy resolve discordant DSD data
items.content:
When karyotype, imaging, and phenotype conflict (Case 2): short-course hCG stimulation tests whether functional Leydig tissue exists — testosterone rise proves testicular androgen capacity even if gonads are nonpalpable. AMH and Müllerian imaging assess Sertoli function and internal duct anatomy.

If non-invasive data remain inconclusive, diagnostic laparoscopy with gonadal biopsy/histopathology is gold standard — distinguishing streak gonad from dysgenetic testis, ruling out ovotesticular DSD (Case 3), and guiding gonadectomy side and sex-of-rearing discussions. Never substitute empiric diagnosis (Case 3 CAH error) for this sequence.

items.title: Five-case comparison table — Swyer vs MGD vs mosaic adult
items.content:
Case 1 (Swyer, 16F): 46,XY, uterus, hypergonadotropic hypogonadism, bilateral streaks → gonadectomy + E/P. Case 2 (MGD, 2F): Prader 2, 45,X mosaic initially, hCG+ testosterone, streak + dysgenetic testis on laparoscopy. Case 3 (MGD, 16F): CAH misdiagnosis on empiric steroids; normal 17-OHP; 46,XY testicular histology only. Case 4 (MGD, 27M): azoospermia, uterus, idic(Yp) mosaic on FISH. Case 5 (CGD, 16F): extreme gonadotropins, unicornuate uterus, 20–30% tumor risk emphasis.

Faculty drill: for each case, identify which node (genetic, gonadal, phenotypic) first diverged and what test proved it.

items.question: Why does a 46,XY individual with Swyer syndrome have a uterus and fallopian tubes?
items.answer: Because gonadal dysgenesis is complete — no functional Sertoli cells produce anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH). Without AMH during fetal life, Müllerian ducts are not regressed and develop into uterus, fallopian tubes, and upper vagina. The karyotype is male (46,XY) but gonadal sex is effectively absent/bilateral streak, so the default internal ductal pathway is female. External genitalia also follow the female pathway without fetal testosterone and DHT.

items.question: How should a 2-year-old with Prader 2 genitalia, initial 45,X mosaic karyotype, and nonpalpable gonads be worked up when CAH is excluded?
items.answer: Continue the glassbox sequence: repeat karyotype/FISH to detect occult Y-lineage cells; measure baseline testosterone, LH, FSH, AMH; perform pelvic ultrasound for Müllerian structures; execute short-course hCG stimulation — a significant testosterone rise (as in Case 2) proves functional testicular tissue despite mosaic 45,X and nonpalpable gonads. If imaging and cytogenetics remain discordant, proceed to diagnostic laparoscopy with histopathology to classify streak vs dysgenetic testis and confirm MGD versus other 46,XY DSD entities.

items.question: Why was empiric glucocorticoid treatment for presumed CAH inappropriate in Case 3?
items.answer: Because foundational biochemical confirmation was never obtained before treatment. Re-evaluation showed normal cortisol and normal 17-hydroxyprogesterone — excluding congenital adrenal hyperplasia. The true diagnosis was 46,XY mixed gonadal dysgenesis, not an adrenal steroidogenic defect. Empiric steroids exposed the patient to lifelong unnecessary glucocorticoid without addressing gonadal dysgenesis, and delayed correct DSD diagnosis and gonadal management.
keyPoints: Empiric steroids without proof — critical error.

items.question: How does isodicentric Y mosaicism produce a phenotypic male with azoospermia and a uterus?
items.answer: Case 4 karyotype: 45,X[44]/46,XY[5]/46,X,idic(Yp)[1]. The dominant 45,X cell line impairs normal gonadal development; the unstable isodicentric Y carries Y material inconsistently across tissues, disrupting coordinated testicular maturation. Partial androgen exposure permitted male external phenotype and male rearing, but gonadal dysgenesis was severe enough to retain Müllerian structures (small uterus on imaging) and abolish spermatogenesis (azoospermia). High-resolution FISH mapped the structural Y abnormality — standard karyotype alone would miss idic(Yp) quantification.

items.stem: The recommended sequential framework for DSD evaluation in this module is:
options: Phenotypic sex → genetic sex → gonadal sex
Genetic sex → gonadal sex → phenotypic sex
items.explanation: Glassbox invariant sequence: karyotype/FISH → gonadal function and anatomy → external/internal phenotype integration.

items.stem: A 16-year-old phenotypic female with primary amenorrhea has 46,XY karyotype, uterus on ultrasound, LH/FSH elevated, and low estradiol. The most likely diagnosis is:
items.explanation: Swyer: 46,XY, Müllerian structures present (no AMH), hypergonadotropic hypogonadism, female phenotype. CAIS has testes producing AMH (no uterus).

items.stem: In Case 2 (Saikia 2019), post-hCG testosterone elevation was clinically important because it:
options: Proved the presence of functional testicular tissue despite initial 45,X mosaic karyotype
items.explanation: hCG-stimulated testosterone rise demonstrated functional Leydig tissue — redirecting diagnosis toward MGD despite mosaic 45,X and nonpalpable gonads.

items.stem: Case 3 was ultimately distinguished from CAH by:
options: Normal cortisol and normal 17-hydroxyprogesterone with 46,XY karyotype
Positive synacthen test for adrenal insufficiency
items.explanation: Normal cortisol and 17-OHP excluded CAH; 46,XY with testicular histology confirmed MGD.

items.stem: Classic mixed gonadal dysgenesis gonadal histology typically shows:
options: A streak gonad on one side and a dysgenetic testis on the other
Bilateral ovotestes with equal ovarian and testicular tissue
items.explanation: MGD hallmark — asymmetric gonads: streak + dysgenetic testis (Case 2 laparoscopy findings).

items.stem: Case 4 (Shetty 2024) required FISH in addition to karyotype because:
options: Standard karyotype may miss quantification of complex mosaicism and isodicentric Y structure
FISH replaces the need for gonadal biopsy
items.explanation: 45,X/46,XY/46,X,idic(Yp) mosaic with structural Y abnormality — FISH maps idic(Yp) and cell-line proportions.

items.stem: In 46,XY complete gonadal dysgenesis, reported gonadal malignancy risk is approximately:
items.explanation: Case 5 (Monolov 2024): ~20–30% develop gonadoblastoma/dysgerminoma — mandates prophylactic gonadectomy.

items.stem: After bilateral gonadectomy in a 16-year-old with 46,XY Swyer syndrome, the next priority is:
options: Cyclical estrogen-progesterone to induce menarche and optimize bone density
items.explanation: Female phenotype and sex of rearing — estrogen-progesterone replacement after gonadectomy (Cases 1 and 5).

items.stem: Which finding best distinguishes Swyer syndrome from complete androgen insensitivity syndrome (CAIS)?
options: Presence of a uterus on imaging
items.explanation: Swyer: no AMH from dysgenetic gonads → uterus present. CAIS: functional testes produce AMH → no uterus.

items.stem: When karyotype, imaging, and hCG results are discordant in infantile DSD, the gold standard for definitive gonadal diagnosis is:
items.explanation: Cases 2 and 3 — laparoscopy with histopathology resolved diagnosis when non-invasive data conflicted.

items.statement: In Swyer syndrome (46,XY complete gonadal dysgenesis), functional Sertoli cells produce sufficient AMH to regress Müllerian structures.
items.explanation: Complete gonadal dysgenesis — no functional Sertoli cells, no AMH, Müllerian structures persist.

items.statement: Empiric glucocorticoid therapy for ambiguous genitalia without measuring 17-hydroxyprogesterone is an acceptable first step.
items.explanation: Case 3 trap — CAH must be biochemically confirmed before steroids; normal 17-OHP excluded CAH.

items.statement: Mixed gonadal dysgenesis often presents with asymmetric gonadal development and mosaic karyotype.
items.explanation: Classic MGD — streak gonad + dysgenetic testis; 45,X/46,XY mosaicism common.

items.statement: Prophylactic gonadectomy is recommended for dysgenetic gonads containing Y-chromosomal material in complete gonadal dysgenesis due to malignancy risk.
items.explanation: ~20–30% malignancy risk — Cases 1 and 5; gonadectomy standard of care.

items.statement: A phenotypic male with azoospermia may have Müllerian structures retained if gonadal development was disrupted by mosaicism and structural Y abnormalities.
items.explanation: Case 4 — small uterus on imaging with 45,X/idic(Yp) mosaic and azoospermia.

items.assertion: A 46,XY individual with Swyer syndrome typically has a uterus and primary amenorrhea.
items.reason: Complete failure of testicular differentiation means no AMH is produced to regress Müllerian ducts during fetal development.
items.explanation: Both true — mechanistic baseline of Case 1.

items.assertion: Laparoscopic gonadal histopathology should be performed when hCG stimulation and initial karyotype provide discordant information in ambiguous genitalia.
items.reason: Histopathology distinguishes streak gonad from dysgenetic testis and confirms MGD when imaging and cytogenetics are inconclusive.
items.explanation: Both true — Saikia 2019 diagnostic algorithm endpoint.
